coredata.dc:title: Strategy to synthesize dual-emission carbon dots and their application for pH variation and hydrogen sulfide sensing and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Liu, Yang
Guo, Qiaozhi
Xu, Hongmei
Jiao, Yuan
Yang, Zhenhua
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, dual emission nitrogen and sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (DE-CDs) were designed for pH variation and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) sensing and bioimaging through fluorescence enhancement. The DE-CDs with green-orange emission were facilely prepared by one-pot hydrothermal strategy using neutral red and sodium 1,4-dinitrobenzene sulfonate as precursors, manifesting intriguing dual-emission behavior at 502 and 562 nm. As the pH increases from 2.0 to 10.2, the fluorescence of DE-CDs gradually increases. The linear ranges are 2.0–3.0 and 5.4–9.6, respectively, which are attributed to the abundant amino groups on the surface of the DE-CDs. Meanwhile, H2S can be employed as an enhancer to increase the fluorescence of DE-CDs. The linear range is 25–500 μM, and the LOD is calculated to be 9.7 μM. Besides, the DE-CDs can be used as imaging agents for pH variation and H2S sensing in living cells and zebrafish due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. All of the results demonstrated that the DE-CDs can monitor pH fluctuations and H2S in aqueous and biological environments, and have promising applications in the fields of fluorescence sensing, disease detection, and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emission carbon dots
pH variation
Hydrogen sulfide sensing
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM image (inset of size distribution); (B) AFM image; (C) FTIR; (D) XPS; (E) UV–vis and PL spectra; (F) excitation-emission matrix of DE-CDs.
(A) Fluorescence titration of different pH; (B) and (C) linear relationship between pH and DE-CDs.
(A) Fluorescence response of DE-CDs in the presence of H2S; (B) linear relationship between H2S and DE-CDs; (C) effects of other anions on the fluorescence intensity of DE-CDs.
LCSM images of DE-CDs at different pH in Hela cells.
LCSM images of DE-CDs and DE-CDs/H2S in Hela cells.
LCSM images of DE-CDs at different pH in zebrafish.
LCSM images of DE-CDs and DE-CDs/H2S in zebrafish.
Fabrication of DE-CDs and their application for pH variation and hydrogen sulfide sensing.

head.ce:title: Strategy to synthesize dual-emission carbon dots and their application for pH variation and hydrogen sulfide sensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of DE-CDs and their application for pH variation and hydrogen sulfide sensing.

In this work, dual emission nitrogen and sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (DE-CDs) were designed for pH variation and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) sensing and bioimaging through fluorescence enhancement. The DE-CDs with green-orange emission were facilely prepared by one-pot hydrothermal strategy using neutral red and sodium 1,4-dinitrobenzene sulfonate as precursors, manifesting intriguing dual-emission behavior at 502 and 562 nm. As the pH increases from 2.0 to 10.2, the fluorescence of DE-CDs gradually increases. The linear ranges are 2.0–3.0 and 5.4–9.6, respectively, which are attributed to the abundant amino groups on the surface of the DE-CDs. Meanwhile, H2S can be employed as an enhancer to increase the fluorescence of DE-CDs. The linear range is 25–500 μM, and the LOD is calculated to be 9.7 μM. Besides, the DE-CDs can be used as imaging agents for pH variation and H2S sensing in living cells and zebrafish due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. All of the results demonstrated that the DE-CDs can monitor pH fluctuations and H2S in aqueous and biological environments, and have promising applications in the fields of fluorescence sensing, disease detection, and bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as new generation of fluorescence nanomaterials, have been widely employed in fluorescence sensing [1–3], bioimaging [4–6], drug delivery [7–9] and other fields due to their excellent photostability [10–12], lower toxicity [13–15] and superior biocompatibility [16–18]. However, the emission of most prepared CDs so far was merely able to give rise to the blue and green emission region, which are hardly avoid the effect of autofluorescence in biosystem [19–21]. Therefore, it is indispensable to exploit preparation strategies for long-wavelength emission CDs (orange to red) [22–24].

Intracellular pH (pHi) plays an important role in physiological processes such as cell proliferation, regulation, ion transport, and cell migration [25–27]. Abnormal levels of pHi can lead to abnormal cell function, growth and division [28–30]. A series of treatment have been developed for detection of pHi, among which the fluorescence method has shown great advantages due to its fast response speed, simple operation and high sensitivity [31–33]. Organic fluorophores, fluorescent proteins, and fluorescent nanomaterials have been reported to detect pHi, but complicated extraction and purification processes hinder their subsequent applications. Therefore, it is crucial to develop fluorescent pHi sensors with facile synthetic methods. The initially fluorescent sensing platform based on blue emission CDs was constructed and used for pH, temperature, and Fe3+ sensing, which provided a fresh perspective for pH sensing [34]. Subsequently, some CDs-based pH sensors were also constructed [35–38]. Unfortunately, most of these CDs have blue or green optimal emission wavelengths, which are not conducive to pHi sensing. Therefore, developing CDs-based pH sensors with long-wavelength emission is a major challenge.

Hydrogen sulfide (H2S), a toxic and flammable gas with a rotten egg smell, has the physiological functions of dilating blood vessels and regulating blood pressure [39–41]. Abnormal levels of H2S can lead to heart disease and high blood pressure [42–44]. Therefore, detection of H2S has an important role in physiological and pathological processes. At present, the developed H2S detection methods all have the disadvantages of complicated operation, low sensitivity and high cost, including colorimetry, gas chromatography and electrochemical methods. The fluorescence method has attracted the attention of researchers due to its advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity and low cost. So far, some organic probes have been developed for the detection of H2S, but they suffer from complex synthetic procedures and poor resistance to photobleaching. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a H2S detection method with excellent optical properties. Yu et al. initially prepared CDs with blue emission for the ratiometric detection of H2S in cells and real samples, and this work provided a new idea for the detection of H2S [45]. Subsequently, some H2S assays based on short-wave emission CDs were also developed, which limited their further biological applications [46–49]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop H2S detection methods based on long-wavelength emitting CDs.

Herein, dual-emitting carbon dots (DE-CDs) with green-orange emission were designed for pH variation and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) sensing (Scheme 1 ). The DE-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using neutral red and 1,4-dinitrobenzenesulfonic acid sodium as carbon and nitrogen sources. The fluorescence of DE-CDs gradually increased with increasing pH based on deprotonation. Besides, H2S can enhance the fluorescence of DE-CDs. The DE-CDs have been successfully used to monitor pH fluctuations and H2S in cells and zebrafish due to their low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. All of the results demonstrated that the DE-CDs can monitor pH fluctuations and H2S in aqueous and biological environments, and have promising applications in the fields of fluorescence sensing, disease detection, and bioimaging.

The proposed DE-CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method. 5.9 mg neutral red (NR) and 11.8 mg 1,4-dinitrobenzenesulfonic acid sodium were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water, then the mixture was transferred and sealed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature. DE-CDs powder were obtained by centrifugation, dialysis and lyophilization.

The sensitivity of DE-CDs to pH environment was probed using Britton-Robinson solutions at different pH values. Using a pH meter, adjust the pH of the Britton-Robinson solution with NaOH (0.1 M). Subsequently, 120 μL of the 0.56 mg/mL DE-CDs solution was dispersed in Britton-Robinson solutions with different pH values. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 502 nm.

The measurement of H2S was performed in 0.01 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.4). Briefly, 120 μL of 0.56 mg/mL DE-CDs solution was added to a colorimetric tube and then diluted to 2 mL with 0.01 M PBS solution (pH = 7.4). Various contents of H2S stock solutions were subsequently added and the corresponding emission spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 502 nm. The selectivity of H2S sensing was evaluated by adding other interfering substances to replace H2S in a similar manner.

For confocal imaging, HeLa cells were seeded on 15 mm glass bottom dishes and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Confocal imaging was performed after incubating HeLa cells with DE-CDs at different pH for 2 h. Additionally, HeLa cells were treated with DE-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 2 h. Then, carefully discard the medium and add fresh medium with H2S. After 30 min of incubation, the treated cells were washed with PBS and visualized using confocal imaging.

The morphologies of DE-CDs were characterized by TEM and AFM. The spherical DE-CDs exhibited a uniform dispersion with an average particle size of 2.73 nm (Fig. 1 A). The lattice spacing of DE-CDs was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. This result was consistent with AFM (Fig. 1B).

The surface structures of DE-CDs were determined by FTIR and XPS spectroscopy. FTIR analysis (Fig. 1C) showed that the vibrational absorption peaks at 3276, 2963, 2836, 1633, 1119 and 598 cm−1 correspond to OH/NH, CH, SH, CN, SOX and CS groups, respectively [50–52]. The four typical peaks of the full XPS spectra (Fig. 1D) are located at 529.3, 398.2, 281.2 and 168.4 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively [53–55]. The two distinct peaks of the C1s spectra at 284.8 and 285.6 eV were attributed to CC/CC and CS (Fig. S1A). In the case of N1s (Fig. S1B), there are four main peaks at 398.9, 399.6, 401.2 and 406.4 eV, ascribed to pyridine N, amino N, pyrrolic N and nitro N, respectively. The S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S1C) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 168.3 eV and 169.5 V, representing the S2p state in -C-SOx-. In the O1s spectrum of Fig. S1D, the peak at about 531.7 eV joined the CO bond, while the peak at 533.4 eV was consonant with CO. These results indicated successful doping of S element.

The unique optical properties of DE-CDs were tested by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. UV–vis spectra showed that DE-CDs had double absorption bands at 258 nm and 528 nm, which were attributed to the π → π* transitions of CC and n → π* transitions of CC (Fig. 1E). Excitation emission spectra showed that DE-CDs had two emission peaks at 502 nm and 562 nm at the excitation wavelength of 378 nm, and the emission wavelength did not change with the excitation wavelength (Fig. 1F). Using Rhodamine B as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 3.5 % (Fig. S2). The stability of DE-CDs was explored by xenon lamp irradiation time and different salt solutions. The fluorescence of DE-CDs did not change significantly after xenon lamp irradiation for up to 60 min (Fig. S3). The fluorescence of DE-CDs remained stable at salt concentrations up to 2 M (Fig. S4).

The fluorescence response of DE-CDs over a wide pH range of 2.0–10.2 was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of DE-CDs gradually increased with the increase of pH. A sigmoidal curve was well fitted using BiDoseResp equation:
y = 6.360 + 6185.17
0.2841
1 +
10
2.628 - x
0.5580
+
0.7159
1 +
10
7.886 - x
0.3547
, R2 = 0.9988 (Fig. S5). Fig. 2B and 2C showed that DE-CDs had two excellent linearities in the pH range of 2.0–3.0 and 5.4–9.6. The pH of the aqueous solution was adjusted between 9.0 and 4.0 using concentrated hydrochloric acid and aqueous sodium hydroxide. After several cycles, the fluorescence intensity of DE-CDs successfully recovered in near neutral conditions (Fig. S6). The DE-CDs have excellent stability at pH 4.0, 7.0 and 9.0 (Fig. S7).

The possible pH response mechanism was explored by zeta potential, dynamic light scattering and fluorescence lifetime. At present, the response mechanisms of CDs to pH are mainly protonation and aggregation-induced emission. The hydrodynamic diameters of DE-CDs (Fig. S8) were 81.66, 28.21 and 190.35 nm at pH 2.0, 7.0 and 9.0, respectively. The hydrodynamic diameter of DE-CDs did not gradually increase with increasing pH, so the pH-responsive mechanism was not ascribed to aggregation-induced emission. With the increase of pH, the zeta potential of DE-CDs gradually decreased (Fig. S9), and the fluorescence lifetime gradually increased (Fig. S10). The N1s spectra indicated the presence of pyrrolic N and pyridine N on the surface of DE-CDs. Since pyridine is more basic than pyrrole, the pyrrole N on the surface of DE-CDs is protonated first with the increase of pH. Therefore, we speculate that the response mechanism of pH is protonation and deprotonation, which pKa are 2.63 and 7.89, respectively (Fig. S11).

Due to the unique functional groups on the surface of DE-CDs, DE-CDs-based H2S probes were designed. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of DE-CDs gradually increased after the introduction of H2S. In the range of 25–500 μM, the concentration of H2S exhibited an excellent linear relationship with F0-F (Fig. 3B, y = 0.8023x-3.645, R2 = 0.9986). The selectivity of DE-CDs was probed by replacing H2S with different metal ions, anions, amino acids and antibiotic. Fig. S12 and Fig. 3C showed that only H2S can enhance the fluorescence of DE-CDs, which proved that DE-CDs could specifically recognize H2S. The effect of H2S on the fluorescence intensity of DE-CDs at different pH was also investigated. Also, we have demonstrated the influence of related intracellular compounds (0.5 mM) on the relative fluorescence intensity of DE-CDs (40 μg·mL − 1) in selectivity test for H2S detection. The results showed that these related intracellular compounds had no effect on the fluorescence of DE-CDs (Fig. S13). After adding H2S, the fluorescence of DE-CDs was significantly enhanced in the pH range of 2.0–7.0, which demonstrated the great potential of DE-CDs in bioimaging (Fig. S14). To our knowledge, this is the first CDs-based fluorescence-enhanced H2S probe (Table S1).

The detection mechanism of H2S was investigated by dynamic light scattering, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime. The hydrodynamic diameter of DE-CDs did not change before and after the addition of H2S, thus expelling aggregation-induced emission (Fig. S15). After the addition of H2S, the UV–vis absorption spectra of DE-CDs changed (Fig. S16). The absorption peak at 258 nm shifted red to 273 nm, and the absorption peak at 528 nm shifted blue to 446 nm, which proved that after adding H2S, DE-CDs combined with H2S to form a new species. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of DE-CDs did not change significantly before and after adding H2S (Fig. S17). Therefore, we speculate that the detection mechanism of H2S by DE-CDs is a static fluorescence enhancement effect.

The cytotoxicity of DE-CDs was explored by MTT (3-(4,5Dimethylthiazol-yl)-2,5Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. Fig. S18 showed that Hela cells cultured in different concentrations of DE-CDs have satisfactory cell viability.

In conclusion, DE-CDs with green-orange emission were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. Based on deprotonation, the fluorescence intensity of DE-CDs gradually increased with increasing pH. Furthermore, H2S can enhance the fluorescence of DE-CDs based on static enhancement. Due to the excellent photostability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of DE-CDs, pH fluctuation and H2S sensing in vivo and in vitro have been successfully achieved, which demonstrated the great potential of DE-CDs in the fields of fluorescence sensing, bioimaging, and disease diagnosis.

Yating Meng: Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yang Liu: Investigation. Qiaozhi Guo: Formal analysis. Hongmei Xu: Project administration, Supervision. Yuan Jiao: Supervision. Zhenhua Yang: Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087, Grants 22104075), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2021Y036), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province, China (No. 20210302124014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122483.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
